1:
‘He went throughout every city and village, preaching and shewing the glad tidings of the kingdom of God: and the twelve were with him.’ The witnesses note Christ’s tireless itinerant preaching.

The fathers draw out the example. Theophylact: he ‘gives us a lesson not to be slothful in teaching’; Gregory Nazianzen: he passes from place to place ‘that he may not only gain many, but may consecrate many places.’ Bede likens him to the eagle raising its young step by step — teaching in synagogues, choosing the Twelve, then taking them with him through the cities.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the sense. Robertson notes the imperfect ‘went about’ (diodeuen, made his way through) and the distributive ‘city and village’; Ellicott marks ‘afterward’ (the word of Luke 1:3) as Luke’s care for sequence. Henry calls him ‘an itinerant preacher… on his circuit.’

The Reformed Calvin notes the Twelve attended ‘as heralds to procure an audience for their Master,’ though the office of teaching was not yet committed to them. The shared, dominant note is that Christ made preaching the kingdom the constant business of his life, going tirelessly from town to town with the Twelve. The witnesses see in his diligence both a pattern for his ministers and the dignity of the gospel errand, the King himself going about as a herald of the kingdom of God. Henry observes that Christ took his work before him and went about it regularly, the end of one good work the beginning of another; the witnesses agree his tireless going from town to town, with the Twelve attending as heralds, sets the pattern for his ministers and displays the dignity of the gospel errand, the King himself proclaiming the kingdom of God. Tertullian notes the rich women who clave to Christ were a subject of prophecy, called by Isaiah to hear his voice; the witnesses see in Christ’s itinerant labour the diffusing of the light of the gospel from city to city. Bede likens Christ to the eagle enticing its young to fly, raising his disciples step by step to heavenly things.
2:
‘And certain women, which had been healed of evil spirits and infirmities… Mary called Magdalene, out of whom went seven devils.’ The witnesses note the women healed by Christ and correct the tradition about Mary Magdalene.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe them. Robertson notes the periphrastic past perfect — they ‘had been healed’ some time before — so all had ‘personal grounds of gratitude’; the ‘seven devils’ mark the special malignity of Mary’s possession. Lightfoot discusses the name Magdalene from Magdala. Barnes, Clarke and Wesley insist there is ‘not the least evidence’ that Mary was a woman of abandoned character or a former prostitute, as tradition and painters suppose — she is here simply one healed of demon-possession.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: these women, healed of evil spirits and diseases, followed and served Christ, and first among them Mary Magdalene, freed from seven demons. The grammatical commentators stress the strong correction of a long-standing error: the seven devils indicate grievous torment and perhaps a deranging madness, not immorality.

The shared, dominant note is the gratitude of the healed. These women, delivered from demons and sickness, attached themselves to the Saviour in thankful devotion; and Mary Magdalene, the witnesses agree, should be honoured not as a reformed harlot — a charge the text nowhere supports — but as one signally delivered by Christ’s power, henceforth a faithful follower who would stand by his cross and be first at his tomb. Lightfoot discusses the derivation of the name Magdalene from Magdala on the Sea of Galilee; the witnesses agree these women, healed of demons and disease, attached themselves to Christ in thankful devotion, and that Mary Magdalene is to be honoured not as a reformed harlot, which the text nowhere supports, but as one signally delivered by his power, henceforth faithful to the cross and the tomb. Robertson notes the presence of seven demons indicates a special malignity and grievous torment; the witnesses agree the women’s gratitude, not any former infamy, is the point, Mary Magdalene a faithful follower delivered by Christ’s power.
3:
‘And Joanna the wife of Chuza Herod’s steward, and Susanna, and many others, which ministered unto him of their substance.’ The witnesses note the women of rank who supported Christ out of their own means.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe them. Barnes and Ellicott note Chuza was steward (manager of income and household) to Herod Antipas, so Joanna was ‘a convert of the upper class,’ the office often held by a trusted servant; Robertson suggests Chuza may be the nobleman of John 4:46-53, and recalls Manaen, Herod’s foster-brother (Acts 13:1). Lightfoot discusses the Idumean name Chuza.

The Reformed Calvin marks the ‘pious zeal’ of matrons of respectable rank, who ‘supply Christ’s expenses out of their own property’ and, not content with that, leave their households to follow him. The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here briefly marks the period of his public ministry.

The shared, dominant note is that women, some of wealth and high station, ministered to Christ and his disciples of their substance. The witnesses see in this both a remarkable mark of the gospel’s reach — even into Herod’s household — and a pattern of grateful, practical devotion: those whom Christ had healed gave of their means to support his work, accounting it an honour to provide for the Master and the Twelve, and teaching the church that what we have is to be held at Christ’s disposal. Robertson recalls Manaen, Herod’s foster-brother (Acts 13:1), as another follower from that court; the witnesses agree that women, some of wealth and rank, ministered to Christ of their substance, a mark of the gospel’s reach even into Herod’s household and a pattern of grateful, practical devotion, holding what they had at Christ’s disposal. Robertson suggests Chuza may be the nobleman of John 4:46-53 whose house believed; the witnesses agree the gospel reached even Herod’s steward’s wife, a convert of the upper class who ministered of her substance. Calvin marks the pious zeal of these matrons, who left the care of their households to follow Christ and supply his expenses. Adam Clarke derives the title ‘steward’ from the keeper or overseer of the king’s household, an office of trust and honour.
4:
‘And when much people were gathered together… he spake by a parable.’ The witnesses note the great crowd and the beginning of the parable of the sower.

The fathers explain the method. Theophylact: the Lord speaks in parables to make hearers attentive and ‘that the unworthy might not receive what was spoken mystically.’ Origen notes ‘not many but few… walk the strait road.’ Eusebius reads the sower as teaching two classes — those who fall from grace through sloth, and those who multiply the seed. Bede holds the Sower is the Son of God, going forth ‘through the vale of the flesh.’

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. Robertson notes this begins the first great group of parables (Mark 4:1-34, Matthew 13:1-53), Luke giving only two; Ellicott allows the parable may have been spoken more than once. Barnes refers to the fuller exposition in Matthew.

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Alexandria block here in fact comments on the sinful woman of Luke 7:36-50, and so does not bear on this verse. The shared, dominant note is that Christ, with a great multitude gathered from every city, taught by the parable of the sower. The witnesses agree the parabolic method both drew and tested his hearers, veiling the mysteries from the careless while opening them to the attentive; and the fathers see in the Sower the Son of God himself, going forth to sow the word in the field of the world. Origen notes that few walk the strait road, and Eusebius that the sower teaches two classes — those who fall through sloth and those who multiply the seed; the witnesses agree the parabolic method both drew and tested his hearers, veiling the mystery from the careless while opening it to the attentive, and the fathers see in the Sower the Son of God himself going forth to sow the word in the field of the world. Theophylact adds that men were accustomed to exercise their minds on dark sayings and despise what was plain, so the Lord spoke in parables; the witnesses agree the method both drew the attentive and veiled the mystery from the careless.
5:
‘A sower went out to sow his seed… some fell by the way side; and it was trodden down, and the fowls of the air devoured it.’ The few voices note the sower and the first of the four soils.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark Luke’s touches. Robertson notes ‘his seed’ is peculiar to Luke, as are ‘was trodden under foot’ and ‘of the heavens’ added to the fowls; Barnes refers to the full exposition of the parable in Matthew 13:3. Gill holds the sower is chiefly Christ, ‘his seed’ best fitting him as proprietor and subject of the word, though it is true of every preacher.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the sower went out to scatter his seed, and some fell on the trodden path, where it was trampled and the birds devoured it. The grammatical commentators note Luke’s distinctive details — ‘his seed,’ the treading underfoot, the fowls ‘of the heavens’ — and Gill that the Sower is pre-eminently Christ.

The shared, dominant note is that the seed of the word, sown broadcast, meets first with the wayside hearer. The path, hardened by traffic and trodden down, gives the seed no entrance, so that it lies exposed and is snatched away. The witnesses see in this the careless, unreceptive hearer, in whom the word finds no lodging and from whom the devil quickly takes it — the first of the four classes the Lord will distinguish, and a warning that the same seed prospers or perishes according to the soil that receives it. Gill holds the sower is chiefly Christ, the proprietor and subject of the word, though it is true of every preacher; the witnesses agree the seed of the word, sown broadcast, meets first with the wayside hearer, in whom the trodden path gives no entrance, so that the word lies exposed and is quickly snatched away — the first of the four classes the Lord will distinguish. Robertson notes ‘his seed’, ‘trodden under foot’, and the fowls ‘of the heavens’ are peculiar to Luke; the witnesses agree the wayside hearer, like the hardened path, gives the word no lodging, so that it is exposed and snatched away.
6:
‘And some fell upon a rock; and as soon as it was sprung up, it withered away, because it lacked moisture.’ The few voices note the rocky soil and Luke’s distinctive detail.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the words. Ellicott notes Luke says ‘upon the rock’ (a more accurate word than the ‘stony ground’ of the others) and that it withered ‘because it lacked moisture’ — the moisture being ‘the dew and rain of God’s grace, without which the seed could not put forth its roots.’ Robertson notes the verbs ‘sprang up’ and ‘withered away.’ Gill reads the rock as hearts hardened by the deceitfulness of sin.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: seed falling on rock sprang up quickly but, lacking moisture and depth, soon withered. The grammatical commentators dwell on Luke’s precise touch — the want of moisture — as adding a spiritual lesson to the want of depth named in the other Gospels.

The shared, dominant note is the shallow, emotional hearer who receives the word quickly but without root. The thin soil over rock gives a rapid but rootless growth, and the first heat withers it; so the word, received with quick joy but no depth, springs up only to die for want of the inward moisture of grace. The witnesses see in Luke’s ‘lacked moisture’ a pointed warning: the swift, superficial reception of the gospel, unwatered by genuine grace and unrooted in a deep heart, cannot endure the heat of trial that the interpretation will name (v.13). Gill reads the rock as hearts hardened through the deceitfulness of sin; the witnesses agree the thin soil over rock gives a rapid but rootless growth that the first heat withers, so that the word received with quick joy but no depth springs up only to die for want of the inward moisture of grace, unable to endure the heat of trial. Robertson notes the verbs ‘sprang up’ and ‘withered away’, the want of moisture being the want of grace; the witnesses agree the shallow heart yields a swift but rootless growth that the heat of trial soon withers.
7:
‘And some fell among thorns; and the thorns sprang up with it, and choked it.’ The few voices note the thorny soil and the fatal companionship of the weeds.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the detail. Ellicott notes the distinctive feature — the thorns ‘grew with its growth, and strengthened with its strength,’ finally overpowering the good seed; Robertson notes ‘grew with it’ (the same root with syn-, together) and ‘choked’ it. Gill reads the thorny ground as hearts filled with worldly cares and sensual pleasures.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: seed falling among thorns grew, but the thorns, springing up together with it, choked it. The grammatical commentators dwell on the deadly simultaneity — the weeds growing up with the wheat and gradually strangling it.

The shared, dominant note is the worldly hearer in whom the word is choked by competing growths. The thorns of care, riches, and pleasure are not absent at the first but spring up alongside the good seed, and by their rank and unchecked growth they smother it before it can bear fruit. The witnesses see in this the peril of a heart that receives the word yet leaves the thorns to grow with it: the gospel is not openly rejected but slowly strangled, the soul divided between the word and the world until, as the interpretation will say (v.14), it brings no fruit to perfection. Gill reads the thorny ground as hearts filled with worldly cares and sensual pleasures; the witnesses agree the thorns of care, riches, and pleasure spring up alongside the good seed and by their rank growth smother it before it can bear fruit, the gospel not openly rejected but slowly strangled in a heart divided between the word and the world. Robertson notes ‘grew with it’ (syn-, together) and ‘choked’; the witnesses agree the deadly simultaneity is the point, the weeds of the world springing up with the wheat and strangling it before it can bear fruit. Ellicott notes the thorns grew with the seed’s growth and strengthened with its strength, finally overpowering it.
8:
‘And other fell on good ground, and sprang up, and bare fruit an hundredfold… He that hath ears to hear, let him hear.’ The witnesses note the good soil and its rich yield.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark Luke’s emphasis. Robertson notes Luke omits the thirty- and sixtyfold of Mark 4:8, dwelling only on the hundredfold, and that ‘he cried’ (imperfect) the warning about hearing; Ellicott marks the same. Gill reads the good ground as honest and good hearts, made so by the Spirit, that receive the word in love. Cyril (in the catena) reads the fruitful ground as the deeply receptive soul.

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is on the gift of the Spirit, tangential. With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: seed on good ground sprang up and bore fruit a hundredfold, and Christ cried aloud the call to hear. The grammatical commentators note Luke’s focus on the highest yield and the loud, earnest summons.

The shared, dominant note is the fruitful hearer whose good and honest heart receives the word, retains it, and brings forth abundant fruit. Against the three failures, the good ground yields a hundredfold — perfect fruit, as the fathers say. The witnesses see in the closing cry, ‘He that hath ears to hear, let him hear,’ the application that searches every hearer: the same seed and the same Sower meet four sorts of heart, and the difference lies not in the word but in the soil that receives it, so that each must take heed how he hears. Cyril reads the fruitful ground as the deeply receptive soul that strikes root and comes to perfection; the witnesses agree the good and honest heart receives the word, retains it, and brings forth a hundredfold, and that the closing cry, ‘He that hath ears to hear, let him hear,’ searches every hearer, the difference lying not in the seed but in the soil that receives it. Robertson notes Luke omits the thirty- and sixtyfold of Mark 4:8, dwelling on the hundredfold, and that Christ cried the warning aloud; the witnesses agree the difference among the four soils lies in the heart, not the seed.
9:
‘And his disciples asked him, saying, What might this parable be?’ The two brief voices note the disciples’ private question.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the imperfect ‘asked’ (eperoton, with the force of repeated, eager questioning), and that the rendering should be ‘what this parable was’ (or meant), the optative being merely the idiom of indirect discourse; Mark adds it was ‘when he was alone’ (Mark 4:10). Gill notes the disciples asked when the multitude was departed. The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced.

With only two brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. The disciples, apart from the crowd, asked Christ the meaning of the parable. The grammatical commentators note the eagerness of their inquiry and that they sought not merely what the parable was but what it signified.

The shared, dominant note is the privilege of the disciples in seeking and receiving explanation. Where the multitude heard only the veiled story, the Twelve came to the Master for its meaning, and were given to understand the mystery. The witnesses see in their question both the proper humility of learners — coming to Christ to be taught what they did not grasp — and the ground of the saying that follows: to them it was given to know the mysteries of the kingdom, because they sought, while the careless, content with the surface, remained without. Robertson notes the eager, repeated questioning of the disciples, perhaps dimly perceiving a reflection on their own growth; the witnesses agree their coming apart to the Master for the meaning is the proper humility of learners, and the ground of the saying that follows — to them it was given to know the mysteries, because they sought, while the careless remained without. Robertson notes the rendering should be ‘what this parable meant’, the optative merely the idiom of indirect discourse; the witnesses agree the disciples sought the meaning when the multitude had departed, a model of the humility of learners. Mark adds that the disciples asked this when he was alone, apart from the multitude that had heard the parable.
10:
‘Unto you it is given to know the mysteries of the kingdom of God: but to others in parables; that seeing they might not see.’ The witnesses note the giving of the mystery to the disciples and its veiling from others.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes part of the mystery here explained is ‘how so many… fail to’ enter the kingdom through ‘manifest unfitness’; the hina (‘that’) shares the truth of Matthew’s causal ‘because’ (Matthew 13:13), for ‘he who hath shall receive more, while he who hath not shall be deprived.’ Ellicott and Barnes note the words are from Isaiah 6:9, given not as a formal quotation. Gill reads ‘others’ as those without, strangers and not children.

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is a polemic against Manes on the blinding of unbelievers, loosely related. The shared, dominant note is that to the disciples it is given to know the mysteries of the kingdom, while to others they remain veiled in parables. The witnesses agree the veiling is at once a judgment and a mercy: a judgment on the willing blindness that ‘seeing sees not,’ yet a mercy that hides from the unworthy what they would only abuse. They see in the saying the solemn principle that the word received yields more light, while the word despised is justly withdrawn — spiritual things being given to those who, like the disciples, seek and receive them. Barnes and Ellicott note the words are from Isaiah 6:9, given not as a formal quotation; the witnesses agree the veiling is at once a judgment on the willing blindness that seeing sees not, and a mercy that hides from the unworthy what they would only abuse, spiritual things being given to those who, like the disciples, seek and receive them. Robertson notes the ‘that’ (hina) shares the truth of Matthew’s causal ‘because’ (Matthew 13:13), for he who hath shall receive more; the witnesses agree the veiling is both judgment and mercy, the mystery given to those who seek. Gill reads ‘others’ as those without, strangers and not children, to whom the doctrines come veiled in parables.
11:
‘Now the parable is this: The seed is the word of God.’ The witnesses note Christ’s own key to the parable.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes the article with both subject and predicate makes them interchangeable — ‘the word of God is the seed’ — and that ‘the word of God’ is a phrase frequent in Luke where Mark has simply ‘the word’ (Mark 4:14). Ellicott takes ‘the word of God’ in its widest sense, ‘every form by which a revelation from God is conveyed.’ Gill notes the seed has God for its author and is made effectual by him.

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here treats the subsistence of the Spirit, tangential. With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ interprets his own parable, declaring the seed to be the word of God.

The shared, dominant note is that the word of God is the seed sown in human hearts. The grammatical commentators stress the identity — word and seed are one — and Luke’s characteristic phrase ‘the word of God,’ taken in its fullest breadth. The witnesses see in this the dignity and the power of the preached word: it is no mere human teaching but God’s own seed, living and effectual, whose fruitfulness depends not on itself but on the heart that receives it — as the four soils, now to be expounded, will show. Ellicott takes ‘the word of God’ in its widest sense, every form by which a revelation is conveyed; the witnesses agree the word of God is the seed sown in human hearts, no mere human teaching but God’s own living and effectual seed, whose fruitfulness depends not on itself but on the heart that receives it, as the four soils now expounded will show. Robertson notes ‘the word of God’ is a phrase frequent in Luke where Mark has simply ‘the word’; the witnesses agree Christ interprets his own parable, the seed being God’s own living and effectual word sown in human hearts. The phrase ‘the word of God’ appears four times in Luke and twelve in Acts, a mark of his usage.
12:
‘Those by the way side are they that hear; then cometh the devil, and taketh away the word out of their hearts, lest they should believe and be saved.’ The witnesses note the wayside hearer and the devil’s theft.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark Luke’s fullness. Ellicott notes Luke uses ‘the devil’ where Mark has ‘Satan’ and Matthew ‘the wicked one,’ and gives the fuller purpose, ‘lest they should believe and be saved.’ Robertson notes it is the devil’s business to snatch up the seed before it sprouts; Barnes ties the taking away to a careless heart (James 1:23-24). Gill reads the wayside as those who hear carelessly, without understanding.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the wayside hearer hears the word, but the devil at once takes it away, lest he believe and be saved. The grammatical commentators note Luke’s distinctive naming of the devil’s aim — to prevent faith and salvation.

The shared, dominant note is that the careless hearing of the unreceptive heart lets the word lie on the surface, where the devil quickly snatches it. The seed never enters; the hardened path gives no lodging; and the enemy, ever watchful, carries off what was sown before it can take root. The witnesses see in Luke’s added clause the whole tragedy made plain: the devil’s express design is to keep men from believing and being saved, so that the careless hearer, by leaving the word unguarded, plays into the hands of the one who would rob him of life. Gill reads the wayside as those who hear carelessly, without understanding; the witnesses agree the careless hearing of the unreceptive heart lets the word lie on the surface, where the ever-watchful devil snatches it before it can take root, his express design, as Luke alone adds, being to keep men from believing and being saved. Ellicott notes Luke uses ‘the devil’ where Mark has ‘Satan’ and Matthew ‘the wicked one’; the witnesses agree the enemy snatches the seed from the careless heart before it can sprout, lest the hearer believe and be saved.
13:
‘They on the rock… receive the word with joy; and these have no root, which for a while believe, and in time of temptation fall away.’ The witnesses note the rootless, temporary hearer.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark Luke’s terms. Ellicott notes ‘in time of temptation’ (better, of trial) is explained by the ‘tribulation or persecution’ of the others, and that Luke gives ‘fall away’ where they give ‘are offended.’ Robertson notes ‘for a while believe’ — an ostensibly sincere start — then ‘fall away’ (stand off, lose interest, drop out). Barnes ties the want of root to a heart unrenewed (Hosea 6:4).

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the rocky-ground hearer receives the word with joy but, having no root, believes only for a season and falls away when trial comes. The grammatical commentators dwell on the reality of the temporary faith and the inevitability of its collapse under pressure.

The shared, dominant note is that quick joy without root cannot endure. The shallow heart welcomes the word gladly, makes a real but superficial start, and for a while believes; but having no depth, when trial or persecution comes it withers and falls away. The witnesses see in this a solemn picture, all too common, of those who embrace the gospel with emotion but without inward rooting, and who, meeting the heat of opposition, lose their first interest and drop away — a warning that joyful reception is not enough without the deep root of genuine grace. Barnes ties the want of root to a heart unrenewed (Hosea 6:4); the witnesses agree quick joy without root cannot endure, the shallow heart welcoming the word gladly and for a while believing, yet, having no depth, withering and falling away when trial or persecution comes — a warning that joyful reception is not enough without the deep root of genuine grace. Robertson notes ‘for a while believe’ describes an ostensibly sincere start that loses interest and drops out; the witnesses agree the rocky-ground hearer, having no root, falls away when the season of trial comes, a warning that joy alone cannot endure.
14:
‘That which fell among thorns are they, which… are choked with cares and riches and pleasures of this life, and bring no fruit to perfection.’ The witnesses note the worldly hearer and Luke’s distinctive vocabulary.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the words. Ellicott notes Luke’s ‘of life’ (bios) and the added ‘pleasures,’ peculiar to him, and his single polished word for ‘bring no fruit to perfection’ (telesphorousin), found nowhere else in the New Testament — a mark of his culture. Robertson notes here the victims themselves are choked, not only the word, both being true (compare 1 Timothy 6:9-10). Gill reads the thorny hearer as one whose heart is filled with worldly cares and lusts.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the thorny-ground hearer hears, but the cares, riches, and pleasures of life choke the word so that it brings no fruit to perfection. The grammatical commentators dwell on Luke’s precise vocabulary and on the double truth — the weeds choke the word, and the worldly heart chokes itself.

The shared, dominant note is the gradual strangling of the word by worldliness. The cares of life, the deceitfulness of riches, and the pleasures of the present age grow up with the word and slowly smother it, so that what began to grow brings no fruit to maturity. The witnesses see in this the peril of the divided heart, which does not openly reject the gospel but lets the thorns of the world flourish beside it until they overpower it — a hearer who, choked by care and pleasure and gain, comes short of the perfected fruit that alone proves the word received in truth. Gill reads the thorny hearer as one whose heart is filled with worldly cares and lusts; the witnesses agree the cares, riches, and pleasures of life grow up with the word and slowly smother it, so that what began to grow brings no fruit to maturity, the divided heart choked by care and pleasure and gain coming short of the perfected fruit that proves the word truly received.
15:
‘But that on the good ground are they, which… having heard the word, keep it, and bring forth fruit with patience.’ The witnesses note the fruitful hearer and the perseverance that marks him.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes ‘an honest and good heart’ (kale kai agathe) is peculiar to Luke, echoing the Greek ideal of the high-minded gentleman, yet adding moral qualities not in the Hellenic phrase. Ellicott renders ‘honest’ as ‘noble’ and ‘patience’ as ‘perseverance,’ something more vigorous than passive submission. Clarke stresses that ‘perseverance’ (so Romans 2:7) shows the good ground continues to bear, the saints persevering in righteousness.

The Wesleyan Wesley sums the contrasts: those who ‘keep it’ (not like the wayside), ‘bring forth fruit’ (not like the thorny), ‘with perseverance’ (not like the stony). The shared, dominant note is that the good-ground hearer receives the word into an honest and good heart, holds it fast, and brings forth fruit with steadfast perseverance.

The witnesses see in this the true and fruitful hearer, distinguished from the other three by a heart made honest and good by the Spirit, by the keeping of the word, and by patient continuance. The fruit is not sudden and short-lived but borne with perseverance to the end; and the lesson, they agree, is that the genuine reception of the word is proved not by quick emotion or fair beginnings but by a deep, retentive heart that holds the word fast and perseveres in bringing forth the fruit of righteousness. Clarke stresses that perseverance (Romans 2:7) shows the good ground continues to bear, the saints persevering in righteousness; the witnesses agree the true hearer receives the word into an honest and good heart, holds it fast, and brings forth fruit with patient continuance, the genuine reception of the word proved not by quick emotion but by a deep, retentive heart that perseveres to the end. Ellicott renders ‘honest’ as ‘noble’ and ‘patience’ as ‘perseverance’, something more vigorous than passive submission; the witnesses agree the genuine reception of the word is proved by a deep, retentive heart that holds it fast and perseveres to the end.
16:
‘No man, when he hath lighted a candle, covereth it… but setteth it on a candlestick, that they which enter in may see the light.’ The witnesses note the lamp set to give light, applied to the gospel and the disciple.

The fathers read it several ways. Bede and Eusebius: the mysteries veiled in parables are not to stay hidden but to be revealed; the lamp is lit to give light. Augustine reads ‘the boldness of preaching’ — none should ‘through fear of fleshly ills conceal the light of knowledge,’ the candlestick being the body submitted to God’s service. Chrysostom: the disciples, exposed to all men’s view, must shine, for ‘so great virtue’ cannot be hid.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the figure. Robertson and Ellicott note Luke’s portable lamp and general ‘vessel’ (where Mark and Matthew have ‘the bushel’), and that the saying, given in the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:15) and after the parable in Mark (Mark 4:21), is here a special application of the interpreting of the parable.

The shared, dominant note is that the light of the gospel, given to the disciples, is not to be hidden but displayed for the good of all who enter. The witnesses see in the lamp both the revealed truth, once veiled in parables and now to be made known, and the disciple himself, set on a stand to shine before men; and the lesson, drawn out by Augustine and Chrysostom, that the knowledge of Christ is given not to be concealed through fear or sloth but to be boldly published, that others may see the light and be saved. Origen notes the lamp must be set on the candlestick, that is, the whole Church, for those who enter; the witnesses agree the light of the gospel, given to the disciples, is not to be hidden but displayed for the good of all who enter, the disciple himself set on a stand to shine, the knowledge of Christ given not to be concealed through fear or sloth but boldly published that others may see the light.
17:
‘For nothing is secret, that shall not be made manifest.’ The few voices note that what is now veiled will be revealed.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes ‘that shall not be known’ is peculiar to Luke; Ellicott marks this as one of the maxims often on Christ’s lips, applied here to the interpreting of the parable, in Matthew 10:26 to the charge to the Twelve, and in Luke 12:2 to the uselessness of hypocrisy. Clarke applies it: ‘whatever I teach you in private, ye shall teach publicly.’

The Wesleyan Wesley draws the point: ‘strive not to conceal it at all; for you can conceal nothing long.’ With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: nothing hidden shall fail to be made manifest, and so the truth now taught the disciples in secret is to be openly proclaimed.

The shared, dominant note is that the gospel, for a time veiled in parables and given privately to the disciples, is destined to be published abroad. The grammatical commentators note the saying’s several applications, but its force here is the disciples’ commission: what they have received in the shade is to be brought into the light. The witnesses see in this both an encouragement to bold proclamation — for the truth cannot finally be hid — and a warning against concealment, whether from fear or hypocrisy, since all things shall at last be made manifest. Clarke applies it: whatever Christ taught in private the disciples shall teach publicly and explain every parable; the witnesses agree the gospel, for a time veiled in parables and given privately, is destined to be published abroad, an encouragement to bold proclamation since the truth cannot finally be hid, and a warning against concealment from fear or hypocrisy. Ellicott marks this as one of the maxims often on Christ’s lips, applied here to the interpreting of the parable; the witnesses agree what the disciples received in secret is to be brought into the light, since nothing hidden can finally remain so. Wesley draws the point: strive not to conceal it at all, for you can conceal nothing long.
18:
‘Take heed therefore how ye hear: for whosoever hath, to him shall be given; and whosoever hath not, from him shall be taken even that which he seemeth to have.’ The witnesses note the charge to hear well and the law of spiritual increase and loss.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes Luke’s ‘how ye hear’ (the manner) where Mark has ‘what ye hear’; both matter. Lightfoot cites the rabbinic saying that God’s measure is unlike man’s — the full vessel receives more, the empty nothing. Clarke and Ellicott press the close: ‘that which he seemeth to have’ is better ‘that which he thinks he hath’ (1 Corinthians 10:12), for the unused knowledge is no real possession and will be lost.

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is a polemic against Manes, loosely related. The shared, dominant note is that men are to take heed how they hear, since to the one who has (and uses) more is given, while from the one who has not even his seeming possession is taken.

The witnesses see in this both an urgent charge and a solemn law. The manner of hearing matters supremely: some things should not be heard, some heard and forgotten, others treasured and obeyed. And the law is fixed — spiritual gifts grow by use and wither by neglect; the hearer who improves what he has receives more, while the careless hearer loses even the unprofitable knowledge he fancied his own. The witnesses agree the verse crowns the parable: the responsibility lies with the hearer, and how he hears decides whether the seed of the word shall increase to a hundredfold or be taken away. Lightfoot cites the rabbinic saying that God’s measure is unlike man’s, the full vessel receiving more and the empty nothing; the witnesses agree the manner of hearing matters supremely, and the law is fixed — spiritual gifts grow by use and wither by neglect, the hearer who improves what he has receiving more while the careless loses even the seeming possession he fancied his own. Clarke and Ellicott press that ‘that which he seemeth to have’ is better ‘that which he thinks he hath’ (1 Corinthians 10:12), the unused knowledge no real possession; the witnesses agree the verse crowns the parable, the hearer’s responsibility deciding whether the seed increases or is taken away.
19:
‘Then came to him his mother and his brethren, and could not come at him for the press.’ The witnesses note the coming of Christ’s kindred and the question of his ‘brethren.’

The fathers probe the kinship. Titus of Bostra: ‘no one in regard of his divine nature is the brother of the Saviour… but he has, by the grace of piety, made us partakers.’ Bede holds the ‘brethren’ are not children of Mary (against Helvidius) nor of Joseph by another wife, but kinsfolk. Chrysostom and Theophylact note the saying is no reproof of Mary but lifts her higher, and that some came mocking his lowly birth.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the order. Robertson observes Mark and Matthew place this visit before the parable, and that the brothers (younger sons of Joseph and Mary, he holds) were not yet openly hostile (John 2:12). Gill names Joses and Simon, since James and Jude were among the Twelve.

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Alexandria block here comments on the sower (a catena offset). The shared, dominant note is that Mary and his kinsmen, unable to reach him for the crowd, sought him as he taught. The witnesses divide over the ‘brethren’ — kinsmen, or sons of Joseph, or (rejected by most) of Mary — but agree the episode sets up Christ’s great saying that true kinship to him is spiritual, belonging to those who hear and do the word of God. Ambrose notes that he ought not to stand without who seeks Christ, and reads the kindred as a figure of the synagogue, the Church preferred before it; the witnesses divide over the ‘brethren’ — kinsmen, or sons of Joseph, or (rejected by most) of Mary — but agree the episode sets up Christ’s great saying that true kinship to him is spiritual, belonging to those who hear and do the word of God. Chrysostom notes the saying was no reproof of Mary but raised her higher, that she might consider him her Lord; the witnesses agree the episode prepares for Christ’s declaration that true kinship to him is spiritual, belonging to those who hear and do the word.
20:
‘And it was told him… Thy mother and thy brethren stand without, desiring to see thee.’ The few brief voices note the message brought to Christ.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes ‘was told’ (the common verb to bring word), and Ellicott that, given Mark’s fuller report, this may be among the facts Luke learnt from Mark at Rome. Gill notes the textual variants in the clause ‘which said.’

With only a few brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. Word was brought to Christ, as he taught, that his mother and brethren stood outside, wishing to see him. The grammatical commentators note the simple report and its relation to the parallels.

The shared, dominant note is that the message of his kindred’s arrival, delivered in the midst of his teaching, becomes the occasion for Christ’s saying on true kinship. The family stood ‘without,’ unable to reach him for the throng of eager hearers; and the announcement, whether kindly meant or, as some of the fathers thought, an attempt to interrupt or shame him, draws from the Lord the great declaration of the next verse. The witnesses see in the scene the setting for a lesson that ranks spiritual relationship above natural — the hearers within, pressing close to the word, being nearer to him than the kinsmen waiting without. Ellicott suggests this may be among the facts Luke learnt from Mark at Rome; the witnesses agree the announcement, delivered in the midst of his teaching, becomes the occasion for Christ’s saying on true kinship, the family standing without while the hearers within, pressing close to the word, prove nearer to him than the kinsmen waiting outside. Robertson notes ‘was told’ is the common verb to bring word or tidings; the witnesses agree the message of his kindred’s arrival, delivered as he taught, becomes the occasion for his saying on true kinship, the family standing without while the hearers press within. Gill notes the textual variants in the clause ‘which said’, some copies reading simply ‘they said unto him’. Ellicott renders it more simply ‘it was told him’, the fuller report belonging to Mark, whom Luke may have met at Rome.
21:
‘My mother and my brethren are these which hear the word of God, and do it.’ The witnesses note Christ’s great word on true, spiritual kinship.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes the absence of the article probably means ‘mother to me and brothers to me are those who hear and do,’ for ‘no one is a child of God because of human parentage’ (John 1:13); ‘family ties are temporal; spiritual ties eternal.’ Ellicott notes ‘the word of God’ here (where Mark has ‘the will of God’) shows the fulness of that phrase. Gill notes he answered those who told him, not Mary herself. The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ declares his true kindred to be those who hear the word of God and do it. The grammatical commentators stress that hearing and doing together — as at the close of the Sermon (Matthew 7:24) — constitute this relationship.

The shared, dominant note is that spiritual kinship to Christ outranks the natural. He does not disown his mother and brethren, but lifts the eyes of all to a higher bond: those who hear and obey the word of God are his true family. The witnesses see in this both the dignity Christ confers on the obedient hearer — nearer to him than blood — and the abiding test of discipleship, that hearing must issue in doing; the parable of the sower thus finds its application, for the good-ground hearer who keeps the word and bears fruit is the very one Christ here calls mother and brother. Robertson notes hearing and doing together, as at the close of the Sermon (Matthew 7:24), constitute this relationship; the witnesses agree Christ does not disown his mother and brethren but lifts all eyes to a higher bond, those who hear and obey the word being his true family, so that the good-ground hearer of the parable is the very one he calls mother and brother. Ellicott notes ‘the word of God’ here, where Mark has ‘the will of God’, shows the fulness of that phrase; the witnesses agree spiritual kinship outranks the natural, the obedient hearer being nearer to Christ than blood, and hearing must issue in doing.
22:
‘Let us go over unto the other side of the lake. And they launched forth.’ The witnesses note the start of the voyage that leads to the stilling of the storm.

The fathers read it spiritually. Cyril notes the Lord exposed the disciples to sea and winds that they too might know his benefits in their own persons. Ambrose: ‘no one can pass from this life without temptations… we must awake the Pilot, who… commands the winds’; he who clings to Christ can never perish. Bede recalls how the risen Lord likewise upbraided the disciples’ unbelief.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. Robertson notes ‘launched forth’ (anechthesan, put out to sea), a nautical term frequent in Luke and Acts; Ellicott marks the crossing from the western to the eastern shore. Barnes refers to the fuller account (Matthew 8:23-24, Mark 4:35-41).

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Alexandria block here comments on Christ’s true kindred (a catena offset), and the Tertullian note answers Marcion on the Creator’s ownership of the elements. The shared, dominant note is that Christ, having taught the multitudes, took ship with his disciples to cross the lake. The witnesses see in the voyage both a true historical event and, with the fathers, a figure of the church’s passage through the storms of this life, in which the disciple, beset by temptation, must wake and cling to the Pilot who commands the winds. Cyril notes the Lord exposed the disciples to sea and winds that they too might know his benefits in their own persons; the witnesses agree Christ, having taught the multitudes, took ship to cross the lake, a true event and, with the fathers, a figure of the church’s passage through the storms of this life, in which the disciple must wake and cling to the Pilot who commands the winds. Ambrose bids us, beset by the storms of spiritual wickedness, awake the Pilot who commands the winds; the witnesses agree the voyage is both a true event and a figure of the church’s passage through this life, in which he who clings to Christ can in no wise perish.
23:
‘But as they sailed he fell asleep: and there came down a storm of wind on the lake; and they were filled with water, and were in jeopardy.’ The witnesses note Christ’s sleep and the sudden, dangerous tempest.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark Luke’s precise words. Robertson notes ‘he fell asleep’ (aphypnosen, possibly a medical use, the only mention of Christ’s sleep) and that the wind-storm (lailaps) literally ‘came down’ through the Jordan gorge upon the lake; Ellicott notes ‘they were filling’ (the process). Clarke parallels Jonah’s tempest (Jonah 1:4), the same word for ‘in jeopardy’ used in the Greek there.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: as they sailed Jesus fell asleep, and a violent squall swept down so that the boat filled and they were in real danger. The grammatical commentators dwell on Luke’s physician’s touch and on the geography of these sudden Galilean storms.

The shared, dominant note is the contrast between the sleeping Saviour and the terrified disciples in mortal peril. His sleep, the only one recorded, marks the reality of his human weariness; the storm, rushing down on the lake, was genuine and deadly, the boat filling with water. The witnesses see in the scene both a witness to Christ’s true manhood — asleep amid the tempest — and the setting for the display of his divine power, the calm of his rest a foil to the disciples’ fear and a quiet token that the Lord of the winds was in no danger though they thought themselves perishing. Clarke parallels Jonah’s tempest (Jonah 1:4), the same Greek word for ‘in jeopardy’; the witnesses agree that as they sailed Jesus fell asleep, his only recorded sleep marking his true human weariness, while a violent squall swept down the Jordan gorge upon the lake so that the boat filled and they were in real danger, the calm of his rest a foil to their fear. Ellicott notes ‘they were filling’ describes the process, not the completion; the witnesses agree the sleeping Saviour and the terrified disciples in mortal peril stand in sharp contrast, his sleep marking his true manhood and the storm a real and deadly squall.
24:
‘Master, master, we perish… Then he arose, and rebuked the wind and the raging of the water: and they ceased, and there was a calm.’ The witnesses note the disciples’ cry and Christ’s word that stilled the storm.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark Luke’s terms. Robertson notes the repeated ‘Master, Master’ (epistata, Luke’s word for the ‘Rabbi’/‘Lord’ of the others) shows their uneasiness, and the present ‘we are perishing’; ‘the raging of the water’ (klydon) is a violent surge. Ellicott notes epistates is Luke’s more classical word for ‘Master’ (Luke 5:5). Barnes ties the awaking to Psalms 44:23.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the terrified disciples woke Christ with the cry ‘we perish,’ and he, rising, rebuked the wind and the surging water, and at once there was a calm. The grammatical commentators dwell on the urgency of the doubled ‘Master’ and the completeness of the sudden calm.

The shared, dominant note is the sovereign authority of Christ over the elements. He does not pray but commands — rebuking the wind and the waves as a master rebukes a servant — and the raging storm instantly obeys, sinking into a great calm. The witnesses see in this a manifest proof of his deity: the same Lord who slept in true human weariness rises and stills with a word the tempest that no man could control, so that the disciples’ cry of despair is answered by a display of power that turns their fear to wonder. Barnes ties the awaking to Psalms 44:23; the witnesses agree the terrified disciples woke Christ with the cry ‘we perish,’ and he, rising, rebuked the wind and the surging water so that at once there was a calm, a manifest proof of his deity, the same Lord who slept in human weariness stilling with a word the tempest no man could control. Ellicott notes epistates is Luke’s more classical word for ‘Master’ (Luke 5:5); the witnesses agree Christ does not pray but commands, rebuking the wind and waves as a master his servant, and the raging storm instantly obeys, sinking into a great calm.
25:
‘Where is your faith?… What manner of man is this! for he commandeth even the winds and water, and they obey him.’ The few voices note Christ’s rebuke of fear and the disciples’ awe.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott corrects the rendering to ‘Who then is this?’ and ‘the water’; the question marks the disciples’ astonishment. The Wesleyan Clarke presses the rebuke: ‘Ye have a power to believe, and yet do not exercise it… Many are looking for more faith without using that which they have.’ Gill notes the words were spoken to the disciples who had professed faith yet showed little.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ chid the disciples for their failed faith, and they, amazed, asked who this could be that even wind and water obey. The grammatical commentators note the force of the question — ‘Who then is this?’ — and Clarke the lesson that faith is to be exercised, not merely wished for.

The shared, dominant note is the joining of rebuke and revelation. Christ’s ‘Where is your faith?’ exposes the disciples’ fear in his presence, for he who clings to Christ need not perish; and their wondering question confesses, even in their amazement, the mystery of his person. The witnesses see in the calmed storm an answer to the question it raised: the one who commands the winds and the sea, and is obeyed, is no mere man but the Lord of creation — and the disciples’ want was not of power to believe but of the will to use the faith they had. Gill notes the words were spoken to the disciples who had professed faith yet showed little; the witnesses agree Christ’s rebuke exposes their fear in his presence, for he who clings to Christ need not perish, while their wondering question confesses the mystery of his person — the one who commands the winds and the sea, and is obeyed, being no mere man but the Lord of creation. Clarke presses that the disciples had a power to believe yet did not exercise it, looking for more faith without using what they had; the witnesses agree the calmed storm answers the question it raised — the one whom wind and water obey is the Lord of creation.
26:
‘And they arrived at the country of the Gadarenes, which is over against Galilee.’ The witnesses note the landing and the famous question of the place-name.

The grammatical-historical commentators face the textual-geographical crux. Robertson holds ‘Gerasenes’ is correct here (as in Mark) and ‘Gadarenes’ in Matthew, the discrepancy cleared by the discovery of Khersa (Gerasa) on the steep eastern bank near Gadara (Matthew 8:28, Mark 5:1). Titus of Bostra argues for ‘Gergesenes,’ from an ancient city by the lake with a rock overhanging it. Calvin notes the man met Christ before he reached the city.

The fathers read it mystically. Bede: Gerasa signifies the Gentile nations, visited by Christ after his passion; Augustine reads the demoniac, bound and broken loose, as a figure of the Gentiles under and beyond the law. The Catena’s Cyril-of-Alexandria block here comments on the storm (a catena offset).

The shared, dominant note is that Christ, crossing the lake, came to the country opposite Galilee, where he would heal the demoniac. The witnesses divide over the exact name — Gadarenes, Gerasenes, or Gergesenes — but agree the textual difficulty is resolved by the geography of the eastern shore, and that the landing in Gentile territory both occasions a great deliverance and, as the fathers read it, foreshadows the gospel’s coming to the nations. Calvin notes the demoniac met Christ before he reached the city, and Augustine reads the bound, broken-loose man as a figure of the Gentiles under and beyond the law; the witnesses divide over the exact name — Gadarenes, Gerasenes, or Gergesenes — but agree the difficulty is resolved by the geography of the eastern shore, the landing in Gentile territory foreshadowing the gospel’s coming to the nations. Robertson holds ‘Gerasenes’ correct here, cleared by the discovery of Khersa on the steep eastern bank near Gadara; the witnesses divide over the exact name but agree the landing in Gentile territory occasions a great deliverance and foreshadows the gospel’s coming to the nations. Bede reads Gerasa, ‘casting out an inhabitant’, as the Gentile nations from whom the devil that possessed them is cast out.
27:
‘There met him out of the city a certain man, which had devils long time, and ware no clothes, neither abode in any house, but in the tombs.’ The witnesses note the wretched state of the demoniac.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark Luke’s detail. Ellicott and Robertson note the physician’s touch: Luke alone records that the man ‘ware no clothes’ (better, no outer garment) ‘for a long time,’ a phenomenon of frenzied insanity Luke would have known; Mark implies it by ‘clothed’ at the cure. Clarke notes such deranged persons still wander naked in some lands. The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: as Christ landed, a man long possessed by demons, naked and dwelling among the tombs, met him. The grammatical commentators dwell on Luke’s precise, clinical description of the man’s degradation — unclothed, homeless, haunting the graves.

The shared, dominant note is the utter ruin wrought by the demons in their victim. Long possessed, stripped of clothing, driven from human society to live among the dead, the man is a picture of the dehumanising power of the evil spirits. The witnesses see in Luke’s unsparing detail both the mark of an accurate observer and the depth of the misery from which Christ was about to deliver him — the more wretched the bondage, the greater the mercy and power of the Saviour who comes, even to the Gentile shore and the place of tombs, to set the captive free. Clarke notes that deranged persons still wander naked in some lands, perishing as they stroll; the witnesses agree the man, long possessed, stripped of clothing, and driven from human society to live among the tombs, is a picture of the dehumanising power of the evil spirits, the depth of his misery setting off the mercy and power of the Saviour who comes even to the place of tombs to set the captive free. Ellicott notes the English ‘ware no clothes’ is stronger than the Greek, better ‘wore no outer garment’, a detail Luke the physician alone records; the witnesses agree the man’s degradation — naked, homeless, among the tombs — shows the depth of the bondage from which Christ would free him.
28:
‘What have I to do with thee, Jesus, thou Son of God most high? I beseech thee, torment me not.’ The witnesses note the demon’s cry of recognition and dread.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes ‘fell down’ (prostrate before him) and ‘the Most High God,’ a title common among heathen (Numbers 24:16) — the demon, not necessarily the man, speaking; the idiom ‘what have I to do with thee?’ (compare Mark 5:7). Clarke suggests the demons were ‘constrained… to acknowledge his power.’ Ellicott notes the exact agreement with Mark’s report.

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is on the witnesses to Christ, tangential. With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the demon, through the man, fell before Jesus and cried out, owning him Son of the Most High God and begging not to be tormented.

The shared, dominant note is that the unclean spirit, in the presence of Christ, is forced to confess his deity and to dread his power. The very demons know and tremble — acknowledging the Son of God most high — while many men deny him; and their plea, ‘torment me not,’ betrays their knowledge of coming judgment. The witnesses see in the scene both a witness to Christ’s divine authority, wrung even from the powers of darkness, and the beginning of a deliverance in which the Lord, on the Gentile shore, masters the legion that had so long ruined a man, that the captive might be restored and the gospel proclaimed. Clarke suggests the demons were constrained to acknowledge Christ’s power yet may have shrunk from naming him; the witnesses agree the unclean spirit, in his presence, is forced to confess his deity and dread his power, the very demons knowing and trembling while many men deny him, their plea ‘torment me not’ betraying their knowledge of coming judgment. Ellicott notes the exact agreement with Mark’s report, both in the single demoniac and the words used; the witnesses agree the unclean spirit is forced to own Christ Son of the Most High God and to dread his power, the beginning of a deliverance on the Gentile shore.
29:
"(For he had commanded the unclean spirit to come out of the man. For oftentimes it had caught him: and he was kept bound with chains and in fetters; and he brake the bands, and was driven of the devil into the wilderness.)" The witnesses note the description of the demoniac's torment, given parenthetically to explain his cry. The grammatical voices stress the violence of the possession. Robertson parses the verbs: "it had seized (synerpake), to lay hold by force... was kept under guard (edesmeueto), to put in chains... breaking the bands asunder (diaresson), the preposition intensifying... was driven (elauneto), peculiar to Luke in this incident." Wesley draws the lesson: "For many times it had caught him — Therefore our compassionate Lord made the more haste to cast him out."

On "into the wilderness," Ellicott notes Luke's distinctive usage: "Better, by the demon, to show that it is still the unclean spirit, and not the great Enemy, that is spoken of. Into the wilderness — the Greek word is plural... St. Luke is the only writer who so uses it." The demon drove the man into the desolate places, away from human society — the mark of the destructive, isolating power of the unclean spirit.

The torment the witnesses read as the picture of the demon's cruelty: the man, possessed for a long time, repeatedly seized, bound with chains and fetters that he broke in his frenzy, driven into the wilderness. The detail of the broken bonds shows the superhuman strength of the possession, which no human restraint could hold; only Christ's command could deliver him.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, a parenthesis explaining the demoniac's condition. The witnesses agree: the unclean spirit had long tormented the man — seizing him by force, breaking the chains and fetters that bound him, driving him into the wilderness — a picture of the destructive, isolating cruelty of the demonic, which no human restraint could hold. Luke, with his characteristic precision, marks that it was the demon (not Satan himself) and uses the plural "deserts" found only in his Gospel. Christ's command to the spirit to come out (the cause of the cry in v.28) sets the deliverance in motion: the compassionate Lord makes haste to free the long-tormented man (Wesley). The verse displays the bondage from which Christ delivers — the superhuman grip of the unclean spirit broken not by chains but by the word of the Son of God. The man whom no fetters could bind is loosed by a word, and the power of the demonic yields to the authority of Christ.
30:
"And Jesus asked him, saying, What is thy name? And he said, Legion: because many devils were entered into him." The witnesses note Christ's question and the demon's answer. Gill explains the purpose of the question: "not out of ignorance in Christ, but for the sake of those that were with him; and partly, that the miserable condition of this man might be the more known; and partly, that his own power might be the more manifest in the dispossession." The omniscient Christ asks not to learn but to reveal the magnitude of the possession to the bystanders.

The grammatical voices note the agreement of the Synoptics. Ellicott: "Legion. Here again St. Mark and St. Luke agree." Robertson refers to the discussion at Mark 5:9. A "legion" was a Roman military unit of some six thousand men — a name expressing the vast multitude of demons that possessed the single man.

The answer "Legion" the witnesses read as disclosing the terrible extent of the man's bondage: not one demon but a host, a whole army of unclean spirits. The revelation magnifies both the man's misery and Christ's power, who with a word would expel the entire legion. The question and answer, asked for the bystanders' sake, make the coming deliverance the more manifest as a work of divine power over the massed forces of darkness.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ asks the demon's name — not from ignorance, but to reveal to the bystanders the magnitude of the possession (Gill) — and receives the answer "Legion," disclosing that not one but a host of demons (a Roman legion numbering thousands) possessed the single man. The Synoptics agree on this striking name (Ellicott). The answer magnifies both the man's terrible misery and the power of Christ, who would expel the entire legion with a word. The verse displays the vast extent of the demonic bondage from which Christ delivers: a whole army of unclean spirits in one man, yet powerless before the Son of God. The question, asked for the witnesses' instruction, sets the stage for a deliverance whose magnitude — a legion cast out at a word — would proclaim the supreme authority of Christ over the massed forces of darkness. Where the powers of hell are legion, the word of Christ is enough.
31:
"And they besought him that he would not command them to go out into the deep." The witnesses note the demons' dread of the abyss. The grammatical voices explain "the deep." Ellicott: "Better, into the abyss. The word is not found in the other Gospels, and it clearly means, not the deep waters of the Galilean lake, but the pit, the bottomless pit of Revelation 9:1-2. The man, identifying himself with the demons, asks for any doom rather than that." Robertson: "Rare old word common in LXX from a privative and bathus (deep)... especially the abode of demons as here and Revelation 9-20."

Clarke supplies background from ancient demonology: "In the Chaldaic philosophy, mention is made of certain material demons, who are permitted to wander about on the earth, and are horribly afraid of being sent into abysses and subterranean places... fearing to be sent into abysses... if even a man threaten to send them thither... it is inexpressible how much they will be affrighted and troubled."

The demons' plea the witnesses read as a confession of Christ's authority over them. The legion of unclean spirits, knowing Christ's power to consign them to the abyss (their place of confinement and torment), beg to be spared that doom — a striking testimony that even the demons recognize and dread the authority of the Son of God. They ask "any doom rather than that" (Ellicott), preferring even the swine to the bottomless pit.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the legion of demons besought Christ not to command them into "the deep" — the abyss, the bottomless pit (Revelation 9), their place of confinement and torment, not the lake's waters (Ellicott, Robertson). The plea is a confession of Christ's authority: the unclean spirits, dreading the doom he could pronounce, beg to be spared it, asking any fate rather than the abyss. The verse displays the terror of the demonic before the Son of God: even a legion of unclean spirits trembles at his power to consign them to the pit, and pleads for respite. Their dread of the abyss (which Clarke illustrates from ancient demonology) testifies that Christ holds authority over the forces of darkness and their final confinement. The demons who tormented the man are themselves in terror before Christ; and their plea proclaims that the day of their consignment to the abyss is in his hand. The Lord who delivers the captive is the judge the demons fear.
32:
"And there was there an herd of many swine feeding on the mountain: and they besought him that he would suffer them to enter into them. And he suffered them." The witnesses note the swine and the demons' request. Gill notes the number from Mark: "about two thousand." Robertson notes Luke's characteristic word: "Luke shows his fondness for adjective hikanos (many) here again, where Mark has megale (great) and Matthew pollon (many)."

Wesley addresses why the demons sought the swine: "Not that they were any easier in the swine than out of them. Had it been so, they would not so soon have dislodged themselves, by destroying the herd." The demons sought the swine not for relief but, the witnesses understand, from their destructive nature and their reluctance to be cast out without some object to possess.

Christ's permission — "he suffered them" — the witnesses note as a deliberate act of divine sovereignty. He permitted the demons to enter the swine, not at their command but for his own purposes: to demonstrate visibly the reality and malignity of the possession, to confirm the man's deliverance, and (by the swine's destruction) to show what the demons would have done to the man himself. The presence of the unclean swine (an unlawful herd for Jews to keep) the witnesses sometimes note as itself significant.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the legion, refused the swine's would the abyss, begged to enter a herd of swine feeding nearby, and Christ permitted them. The demons sought the swine not for relief (for they soon destroyed the herd, Wesley) but from their destructive nature. Christ's "he suffered them" is a deliberate act of sovereignty: he permitted the entry for his own purposes — to make the reality and malignity of the possession visible, to confirm the man's deliverance, and to show by the swine's destruction what the demons would have done to the man. The verse displays Christ's authority even in granting the demons' request: they can do nothing without his permission, and what he permits serves his ends. The destruction of the swine (an unlawful herd for Jews) proclaims the murderous intent of the demons, from which the man was delivered, and the sovereign power of Christ, who holds even the unclean spirits on a leash. The Lord governs the very malice of hell.
33:
"Then went the devils out of the man, and entered into the swine: and the herd ran violently down a steep place into the lake, and were choked." The witnesses note the demons' transfer and the swine's destruction, and reject a rationalist misreading. The grammatical voices note the vivid verbs. Robertson: "Rushed down the steep (hormesen kata tou kremnou)... the same vivid verb in each account, to hurl impetuously, to rush. Were choked... second aorist passive." Ellicott: "Better, down the cliff."

A genuine debate concerns a rationalist explanation. Clarke firmly rejects it: "Some critics would have us understand all this of the man himself, who, they say, was a most outrageous maniac; and that, being permitted by our Lord, he ran after the swine, and drove them all down a precipice into the sea! This is solemn trifling indeed... The devils went out of the man, and entered into the swine; i.e. the madman ran after the swine! On this plan of interpretation there is nothing certain in the word of God... Such comments are intolerable." The text plainly states a real transfer of the demons, not the man's frenzy.

The destruction of the swine the witnesses read as the visible confirmation of the deliverance and the proof of the demons' malignity. The same destructive power that drove the man into the wilderness now hurled the swine to their death — showing what the demons intend for all they possess, and confirming that the man was truly freed.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the demons went out of the man and entered the swine, which rushed down the cliff into the lake and were drowned — the vivid verbs (rushed, choked) marking the violence (Robertson). Clarke firmly rejects the rationalist misreading that would make this merely the maniac driving the swine: the text plainly states a real transfer of the demons, and to explain it away leaves "nothing certain in the word of God." The destruction of the swine confirms the deliverance and proves the demons' malignity: the same destructive power that drove the man into the wilderness now hurled the swine to their death, showing what the demons intend for all they possess. The verse displays the reality of the demonic deliverance and its visible confirmation: the man is freed, and the demons, permitted the swine, at once destroy them — a vivid proof of the murderous intent from which Christ rescued the man, and a rebuke to every attempt to explain away the supernatural in the Gospel record.
34:
"When they that fed them saw what was done, they fled, and went and told it in the city and in the country." The witnesses note the swineherds' flight and report. The grammatical voices address a textual point. Clarke: "They went, is omitted by almost every MS. of repute... Griesbach leaves it out, and with propriety too, as it is not likely that so correct a writer as Luke would say, They fled, and Went and told it." Ellicott notes "in the country" is better "in the farms" (plural).

Robertson notes Luke's neat Greek: "Saw what had come to pass (idontes to gegonos). This item only in Luke. Note the neat Greek idiom, articular second perfect active participle." The keepers of the swine, seeing the destruction of their herd and the deliverance of the man, fled in alarm and spread the report through city and farms.

The swineherds' report the witnesses read as the means by which the news of the miracle spread. Their flight and telling brought the whole multitude out to see (v.35), so that the deliverance, witnessed and reported, became widely known. The report, driven by alarm at the loss of the swine, served to publish the work of Christ — though it would also issue in the Gadarenes' fearful rejection (v.37).

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the swineherds, seeing the destruction of their herd and the deliverance of the man, fled and reported it through the city and the farms — the news of the miracle spreading by their alarmed telling. The grammatical voices note the textual and stylistic details (the better reading, Luke's neat Greek). The report brought the whole multitude out to see (v.35), publishing the work of Christ widely, though it would issue in the Gadarenes' fearful rejection (v.37). The verse displays the spreading of the news of the deliverance: the keepers, driven by the loss of their swine, become the unwitting heralds of Christ's power, carrying the report to city and country. The miracle, witnessed and told, becomes known throughout the region — preparing both the crowd's coming and the tragic response of a people who would prefer their swine to the Saviour. The deliverance of one man, published abroad, confronts a whole region with the power and presence of Christ.
35:
"Then they went out to see what was done; and came to Jesus, and found the man, out of whom the devils were departed, sitting at the feet of Jesus, clothed, and in his right mind: and they were afraid." The witnesses note the transformed demoniac and the crowd's fear. Ellicott marks the Lukan detail: "Sitting at the feet of Jesus. This feature is peculiar to St. Luke's narrative. The demoniac was now in the same attitude of rapt attention as that in which we find afterwards Mary the sister of Lazarus (Luke 10:39)." Barnes notes "right mind" recalls the renewal of Psalm 51:10.

The transformation the witnesses read as the visible evidence of the deliverance. The man who had been naked (v.27), driven into the wilderness, breaking his chains, is now "clothed, and in his right mind, sitting at the feet of Jesus" — the very picture of restored humanity, the disciple's posture of attentive reverence. The contrast between the former frenzy and the present calm proclaims the completeness of Christ's work.

The crowd's fear the witnesses note as a mixed and ominous response. They were afraid — not with the holy awe that leads to faith, but with a dread that would issue in their asking Christ to depart (v.37). The very evidence of the miracle, which should have drawn them to the Healer, filled them with fear at his power.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the crowd found the delivered man "clothed, and in his right mind, sitting at the feet of Jesus" — the picture of restored humanity and the disciple's posture of rapt attention (peculiar to Luke, recalling Mary at Christ's feet, Ellicott). The contrast between his former frenzy (naked, chained, driven to the wilderness) and his present calm proclaims the completeness of Christ's work. Yet the crowd "were afraid" — not with holy awe that leads to faith, but with a dread that would issue in their asking Christ to depart (v.37). The verse displays the transforming power of Christ and the tragic response of unbelief: the delivered man sits restored at the Saviour's feet, but the crowd, confronted with such power, is filled not with worship but with fear. The same miracle that restored one man to his right mind drives a fearful people from the Healer — a sobering picture of how the evidence of Christ's power may harden as well as save. The clothed and calm demoniac is the trophy of grace; the frightened crowd, the warning of unbelief.
36:
"They also which saw it told them by what means he that was possessed of the devils was healed." The witnesses note the eyewitnesses' report of the manner of the cure. Gill identifies them: "the disciples of Christ, or the men of the ship, or persons who lived hard by in the fields, who were eyewitnesses." Robertson: "He that was possessed with devils (only two words in Greek, the demonized). Was made whole (esothe, first aorist passive of sozo, to save). This is additional information to the news carried to them in verse 34."

On "by what means," Ellicott: "Better, how; stress being laid on the manner rather than the instrumentality." The eyewitnesses related not merely that the man was healed but how — the casting out of the legion, the entry into the swine, the visible deliverance — so that the manner of the miracle was made known.

The report the witnesses read as the confirming testimony to the deliverance. Those who had seen the whole event told the gathering crowd how the demoniac had been saved (sozo, the word for salvation) — the eyewitness testimony establishing the reality and manner of the miracle. The repeated telling (v.34, v.36) underscores the public, well-attested nature of Christ's work.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the eyewitnesses (disciples, sailors, or those nearby) told the gathering crowd how the demoniac had been "made whole" (the word for salvation, sozo, Robertson) — relating not merely the fact but the manner of the cure (Ellicott). The report confirms the reality and manner of the deliverance, establishing it as a public, well-attested miracle. The verse displays the confirming testimony of those who saw: the manner of the casting out, the entry into the swine, the visible restoration — all related to the crowd, so that the work of Christ was made fully known. The repeated telling (the swineherds in v.34, the eyewitnesses here) underscores how thoroughly the deliverance was attested. Yet the well-established miracle, far from drawing the people to faith, only deepened their fear and led to their rejection (v.37) — a reminder that the clearest evidence of Christ's power does not, of itself, produce faith in hearts unwilling to receive him. The witnesses tell how the man was saved; the crowd asks the Saviour to leave.
37:
"Then the whole multitude of the country of the Gadarenes round about besought him to depart from them; for they were taken with great fear: and he went up into the ship, and returned back again." The witnesses note the Gadarenes' tragic rejection of Christ. The grammatical voices stress the fear. Robertson: "Were holden with great fear (phobo megalo suneichonto). Imperfect passive... with the instrumental case of phobos." Ellicott: "Better, they were oppressed." The whole multitude, gripped by fear, asked Christ to leave.

The rejection the witnesses read as the preference of swine over the Saviour. The Gadarenes, more concerned for their property (the lost swine) than for the deliverance of a man and the presence of the Healer, asked Christ to depart. Their fear was not the awe that worships but the dread that drives away — a fear born of unbelief and worldliness, valuing their material loss above the gift of God.

Christ's response the witnesses note as a solemn judgment. He went into the ship and returned, leaving them as they asked.
