Henry (under v.40): "If some will not accept the favours Christ offers them, others will." Christ does not force himself on those who reject him; he departs, leaving the Gadarenes to the fate they chose. The contrast with the Galileans who "gladly received him" (v.40) is sharp.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the Gadarenes, gripped by great fear, besought Christ to depart — and he went, leaving them as they asked. Their rejection is the tragic preference of swine over the Saviour: more concerned for their lost property than for the deliverance of a man and the presence of the Healer, they valued material loss above the gift of God. Their fear was not worshipping awe but the dread of unbelief and worldliness that drives Christ away. The verse displays the solemn judgment of a people who reject Christ: he does not force himself upon them but departs, leaving them to the choice they made. The contrast with the Galileans who "gladly received him" (v.40) is sharp and instructive. The Gadarenes' request — that Christ leave their coasts — is one of the saddest in the Gospels: confronted with the power that delivers, they chose their swine and sent the Saviour away. To prefer the world's goods to Christ's presence is to lose him; and Christ honours the dreadful liberty of those who will not have him near.
38:
"Now the man out of whom the devils were departed besought him that he might be with him: but Jesus sent him away, saying." The witnesses note the delivered man's desire to follow Christ, in stark contrast to the Gadarenes' rejection. Robertson: "Prayed him (edeeito autou). Imperfect middle, kept on begging." The man, grateful for his deliverance, earnestly and repeatedly begged to remain with Christ.

The Reformed Calvin draws the contrast: "The Gadarenes cannot endure to have Christ among them, but he who has been delivered from the devil is desirous to leave his own country and follow him. Hence we learn how wide is the difference between the knowledge of the wrath and the knowledge of the grace of God. The former strikes men with terror, makes them fly from the presence of God... but the latter draws them gently, and makes them feel that nothing is more desirable than to be united to God." He explains why Christ refused: "that he expected the man to make himself more extensively useful by communicating to his Gentile countrymen the remarkable act of kindness which he had received."

Christ's sending him away the witnesses read not as a rejection but as a commission. Unlike others whom Christ called to follow, this man is sent back to his own people as a witness — the first missionary to the Gentile Decapolis. His desire to be with Christ is honoured not by attendance but by a greater service: to publish the deliverance among his own.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the delivered man, in stark contrast to the Gadarenes who sent Christ away, earnestly begged to remain with him (kept begging, Robertson). Calvin draws the lesson: the difference between the knowledge of God's wrath (which drives men away in terror) and the knowledge of his grace (which draws them gently to desire union with God) — the Gadarenes fled, the delivered man longed to stay. Christ's sending him away is not rejection but commission: unlike others called to follow, this man is sent back to his own people as the first witness to the Gentile Decapolis (v.39). His desire for Christ's company is honoured by a greater service — to publish his deliverance among his own. The verse displays the response of grace against the response of fear: the one who has tasted Christ's mercy cannot bear to leave him, yet is sent to serve. The man who would be with Christ is made a herald of Christ; and his longing for the Saviour's presence becomes the means of carrying the Saviour's name to a region that had rejected him.
39:
"Return to thine own house, and shew how great things God hath done unto thee. And he went his way, and published throughout the whole city how great things Jesus had done unto him." The witnesses note the commission and its fulfilment, and a notable detail of Christ's deity. Gill notes the man went throughout Gadara publishing the deliverance; Robertson: "He had a great story to tell and he told it with power. The rescue missions in our cities can match this incident with cases of great sinners who have made witnesses for Christ."

A striking detail the witnesses observe: Christ said "shew how great things God hath done," but the man "published how great things Jesus had done." The man rightly identified Jesus with God — the deliverance done by Christ being the work of God. Calvin notes Christ's pedagogical method: "He bids him show the work of God... that he may be acknowledged to be the true minister and prophet of God... In this gradual manner it was proper to instruct an ignorant people who were not yet acquainted with his divinity."

Calvin draws the anagogical (spiritual) lesson: "In the person of one man Christ has exhibited to us proof of his grace which is extended to all mankind. Though we are not tortured by the devil, yet he holds us as his slaves... Naked, torn, and disfigured, we wander about, till he restores us to soundness of mind." The bodily deliverance figures the spiritual deliverance of all whom Christ frees from Satan's tyranny.

The witnesses agree: Christ commissioned the delivered man to return home and publish "how great things God hath done unto thee" — and the man published "how great things Jesus had done," rightly identifying Christ with God (the deliverance of Christ being the work of God). He became the first witness to the Gentile Decapolis, telling his great story with power. The verse displays both the commission of the delivered to be witnesses and a quiet testimony to Christ's deity: the work commanded as God's is told as Jesus's, and rightly so. Calvin draws the spiritual lesson: every soul, though not tortured by demons, is held by Satan as a slave — naked, torn, and disfigured — until Christ restores it; and gratitude for so great a deliverance should make us, like the man, publish abroad the great things Christ has done. The trophy of grace becomes the herald of grace; and the man who would have stayed with Christ carries Christ's name through the whole city.
40:
"And it came to pass, that, when Jesus was returned, the people gladly received him: for they were all waiting for him." The witnesses note the glad welcome of the Galileans and the rich allegory of the two intertwined miracles that follow. The grammatical and puritan voices stress the contrast with the Gadarenes. Henry: "Christ was driven away by the Gadarenes... But when he had crossed the water, and returned to the Galileans, they gladly received him... If some will not accept the favours Christ offers them, others will." The people waited and welcomed him with their whole hearts.

The fathers read the intertwined miracles (Jairus's daughter and the woman with the issue) allegorically. Ambrose and Bede: Jairus, the ruler of the synagogue, signifies Moses or the Law; his twelve-year-old dying daughter signifies the Synagogue; the woman with the twelve-year issue of blood signifies the Gentile Church. Bede: "this daughter of the ruler was dying at twelve years, and the woman was afflicted with the issue of blood for twelve years, that it might be understood that as long as the Synagogue flourished the Church was weak." The Gentile Church, "perishing by its falling away," seized first the health prepared for Israel (Ambrose). Cyril notes the throng as a sign of the incarnation: "He had really put on our flesh... they thronged Him."

The historical narrative the witnesses also draw out. Augustine harmonizes the chronology with Matthew's feast of the publicans. Chrysostom notes Jairus's "dullness of heart" in thinking Christ must come in person (unlike the centurion), yet Christ "calmly undertakes the work, knowing that what was to happen would be greater than what was asked" — a resurrection, implanting "a firm hope of the resurrection."

The witnesses agree: the Galileans gladly received Christ on his return, in glad contrast to the Gadarenes who drove him away — if some reject Christ, others welcome him (Henry). The verse opens the intertwined miracles of Jairus's daughter and the woman with the issue of blood, which the fathers read allegorically: Jairus/the Law, his dying daughter/the Synagogue, the bleeding woman/the Gentile Church — the twelve years of each marking that while the Synagogue flourished the Church was weak, until the Gentiles seized the health prepared for Israel. Christ, undertaking Jairus's request, knew he would do more than was asked: raise the dead and implant the hope of resurrection (Chrysostom). The verse displays the welcome of faith and the rich typology of the gospel's passage from Synagogue to Church — Christ moving among a thronging people, about to manifest his power over disease and death, and to figure in two healings the whole drama of Israel and the nations.
41:
"And, behold, there came a man named Jairus, and he was a ruler of the synagogue: and he fell down at Jesus' feet, and besought him that he would come into his house." The witnesses note Jairus's humble, urgent approach. The fathers (under v.40) read his rank and posture. Titus Bostrensis: "there came not one of the lowest, but a ruler of the synagogue, that the mouths of the Jews might be the more closed... he fell at His feet" through urgent need. Chrysostom marks his weak faith: "his dullness of heart... being ignorant that He was able to heal when absent. For if he had known, he would have said as the centurion did, Speak the word."

The puritan Henry draws out the humility and importunity: "Jairus, though a ruler, fell down at Jesus's feet, as owning him to be a ruler above him. It was very importunate... But Christ complied with his request; he went along with him. Strong faith shall be applauded, and yet weak faith shall not be rejected. In the houses where sickness and death are, it is very desirable to have the presence of Christ."

The grammatical voices note Luke's usage. Robertson: "Was (uperchen). Imperfect of uparcho in sense of en... Common in Luke and Acts, but not in other Gospels." Jairus, a man of rank and authority in the synagogue, humbled himself before Christ in his need, falling at his feet and begging him to come.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Jairus, a ruler of the synagogue, came humbly and urgently to Christ — falling at his feet (owning Christ as a ruler above him, Henry) and begging him to come to his house. The fathers note his rank closed the mouths of the Jews (a ruler, not one of the lowest, sought Christ, Titus Bostrensis), yet mark his weak faith in thinking Christ must come in person, unlike the centurion who trusted a word (Chrysostom). Christ complies: strong faith is applauded, yet weak faith is not rejected (Henry). The verse displays the humbling of the great before Christ and the condescension of Christ to the weak in faith: a synagogue ruler at the Saviour's feet, and a Saviour who honours even an imperfect, importunate faith. Jairus's need drove him to Christ; and Christ, who could have healed at a distance, graciously goes with him — meeting the man where his faith was, and preparing to do far more than was asked. The presence of Christ is desirable in every house of sickness and death.
42:
"For he had one only daughter, about twelve years of age, and she lay a dying. But as he went the people thronged him." The witnesses note the dying only daughter and the pressing crowd. The grammatical voices mark Luke's precision. Ellicott: "St. Luke, as with the precision of a practised writer, names the age at the beginning of the narrative, St. Mark incidentally at its close." Robertson: "An only daughter (thugater monogenes). The same adjective used of the widow's son (7:12) and of Jesus (John 1:18; 3:16). She lay a dying — imperfect active, she was dying."

The fathers (under v.40) read the dying daughter allegorically as the Synagogue, "at twelve years of age, that is, when the time of puberty was approaching, lay dying; for having been brought up nobly by the prophets, as soon as it came to years of discretion... it forgot to enter the way of spiritual life, and unless Christ had come to its help, would have fallen away into destruction" (Bede). The Greek Exposition: "the prop of his house, the succession of his race, in the very flower of her age."

The throng the witnesses note both historically and allegorically. Robertson: "Thronged (sunepnigon)... the verb used of the thorns choking the growing grain. It was a jam." Bede (under v.40): the Lord "going to heal the girl is thronged by the crowd, because giving wholesome warnings to the Jewish nation, He was borne down by the customs of a carnal people."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Jairus's only daughter, about twelve, lay dying — Luke with a physician's precision naming her age at the start (Ellicott), and using "only" (monogenes) as of the widow's son and of Christ himself (Robertson). The fathers read her allegorically as the dying Synagogue, nobly raised by the prophets but perishing as it came to maturity, until Christ should help it (Bede). The pressing crowd "thronged" Christ — the word of thorns choking grain (Robertson), figuring the carnal people who pressed but did not believe (Bede). The verse displays the urgency of the case — a beloved only child at the point of death — and, in the patristic reading, the peril of the Synagogue. Christ moves toward the dying girl through a thronging crowd, about to do more than heal: to raise the dead. The dying daughter, the prop of her father's house, is the occasion for the manifestation of Christ's power over death itself; and the throng that presses him is the setting for the secret faith of the bleeding woman (vv.43-48), whose touch would interrupt the journey to the deathbed.
43:
"And a woman having an issue of blood twelve years, which had spent all her living upon physicians, neither could be healed of any." The witnesses note the desperate case of the bleeding woman and Luke's gentler treatment of the physicians. The grammatical voices observe the physician-Evangelist's restraint. Ellicott: "while St. Luke records the failure of the physicians to heal the woman, he does not add, as St. Mark does, that she rather grew worse." Robertson: "He omitted also what Mark has: and was nothing bettered but rather grew worse" — Luke, himself a physician, "takes care of the physicians by the simple statement that it was a chronic case."

The fathers (under v.40) read the woman allegorically as the Gentile Church. Bede: "the issue of blood may be taken for the prostitution of idolatry, and for those things which are done for the delights of the flesh." On the physicians: "either false doctors, or philosophers and teachers of secular laws... or the unclean spirits themselves, who by giving as it were advice to men, procure themselves to be worshipped as God." The Gentiles, like the woman, "had lost all the gifts of nature" on vain remedies.

The twelve years the witnesses (with Bede, under v.40) connect to the twelve years of the dying daughter: while the Synagogue flourished, the Gentile world languished in idolatry and sin, incurable by all human remedies, until Christ came.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the woman had suffered a chronic issue of blood for twelve years, having spent all her living on physicians who could not heal her — Luke, himself a physician, treating the doctors gently (omitting Mark's "grew worse," merely noting it was incurable, Ellicott, Robertson). The fathers read her allegorically as the Gentile Church, defiled by idolatry and fleshly sin, who had wasted all on false remedies (false teachers, philosophers, or the demons themselves) yet remained incurable until Christ (Bede). The twelve years parallel the dying daughter's twelve years: while the Synagogue flourished, the Gentiles languished. The verse displays a case beyond all human help — twelve years of suffering, all means exhausted, no physician able to cure — the very picture of human helplessness that Christ alone can remedy. The woman's desperate, incurable condition sets the stage for the power of Christ, which would heal in an instant what years and fortunes could not. Where all earthly remedies fail, the touch of Christ avails; the incurable finds her cure in him.
44:
"Came behind him, and touched the border of his garment: and immediately her issue of blood stanched." The witnesses note the woman's secret, faith-filled touch and her instant cure. Gill: "Came behind him, in the press and crowd of people, being ashamed to come before him, and tell him her case." Robertson: "The border of his garment (tou kraspedou). Probably the tassel of the overgarment... Stanched (este), second aorist active indicative, stopped at once (effective aorist)."

The fathers (under v.40) read the touch as the act of faith. Ambrose: "in faith she came, in piety believed, in wisdom knew herself to be healed... From modesty then she touched the hem of His garment." Theophylact: "he who brings faith to Him who is able to cure, immediately obtains his cure... first touched Christ with her mind, then with her body." Chrysostom: "not the garments alone saved her... but the earnestness of her faith."

The instant cure the witnesses read as the reward of faith. The woman, ashamed of her unclean condition (which by the Law made her touch defiling), came secretly behind and touched only the tassel of Christ's garment — yet her faith drew healing virtue, and the issue stopped at once. The cure was not magic in the garment but the response of Christ's power to her faith.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the woman, ashamed of her unclean condition, came secretly behind Christ and touched only the tassel of his garment — and her issue of blood stopped at once (the effective aorist, Robertson). The fathers read the touch as the act of faith: she touched Christ first with her mind, then with her body (Theophylact); not the garment but her earnest faith saved her (Chrysostom). The instant cure is the reward of faith — the woman drawing healing virtue not by magic in the cloth but by the response of Christ's power to her believing touch. The verse displays the power of faith that reaches Christ even in the press of a crowd: the secret, trembling touch of a desperate woman avails where twelve years of physicians failed. Her modesty and her faith together — coming behind, touching the hem, believing she would be healed — drew from Christ an instant and complete cure. The trembling touch of faith, however hidden, does not escape the power or the notice (v.45) of the Saviour; and the incurable is healed in a moment by the touch of the Lord.
45:
"And Jesus said, Who touched me? When all denied, Peter and they that were with him said, Master, the multitude throng thee and press thee, and sayest thou, Who touched me?" The witnesses note Christ's question and the disciples' puzzled reply. Gill notes the purpose: Christ asked "not as ignorant of the person... but in order to discover her to the people, and the cure she had received, as well as her faith." The omniscient Christ asks not to learn but to bring the secret healing to light.

The Reformed Calvin addresses the apparent difficulty: "It does appear absurd that Christ should pour out his grace without knowing on whom he was bestowing a favour... Beyond all question, he knowingly and willingly cured the woman... but he puts the question to her, that she may freely and publicly make it known. If Christ had been the only witness of his miracle, his statements might not perhaps have been believed: but now, when the woman, struck with dread, relates what happened to her, greater weight is due to her confession."

The disciples' reply the witnesses note as missing the distinction Christ drew. Robertson: "Press thee and crush thee (sunechousin se kai apothlibousin)... Crush thee, a verb used of pressing out grapes." Peter and the others, seeing only the physical thronging, wondered at the question; but Christ distinguished the touch of faith from the mere pressure of the crowd (v.46). The Greek Exposition (under v.40): "it is not truly touching if a man touch unfaithfully."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ asked "Who touched me?" — not from ignorance, but to bring the secret healing and the woman's faith to light (Gill). Calvin resolves the difficulty: Christ knowingly cured her, but asked the question that she might publicly confess, lest the miracle rest on his testimony alone; the woman's own trembling confession gives it greater weight. The disciples, seeing only the physical throng pressing and crushing him (Robertson), missed the distinction Christ drew between the mere pressure of the crowd and the touch of faith (v.46) — for it is not truly touching to touch unfaithfully (Greek Exposition). The verse displays Christ's purpose in drawing out the hidden faith: the secret cure must become a public testimony, both to confirm the miracle and to honour the woman's faith. Many throng Christ; one touches him. The crowd presses without faith and receives nothing; the woman touches in faith and is healed. Christ distinguishes the believing touch from the careless press, and will not let the faith that healed her remain hidden.
46:
"And Jesus said, Somebody hath touched me: for I perceive that virtue is gone out of me." The witnesses note Christ's distinction of the believing touch and the going forth of healing power. Gill: "Not in a common and accidental way, but with design, and in the strength of faith." Christ distinguishes the one purposeful, faith-filled touch from the many accidental contacts of the crowd.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke draws the lesson on faith: "This Divine emanation did not proceed always from Christ, as necessarily as odours do from plants, for then all who touched him must have been equally partakers of it. Of the many that touched him, this woman and none else received this Divine virtue; and why? Because she came in faith. Faith alone attracts and receives the energetic influence of God at all times. There would be more miracles, at least of spiritual healing, were there more faith among those who are called believers."

On "virtue is gone out of me," the grammatical voices note Luke's medical interest. Ellicott: "To St. Luke the word was probably familiar as a technical term." Robertson: "Jesus felt the sensation of power already gone. Who does not know what this sense of goneness or exhaustion of nervous energy means?" The healing power went forth from Christ in response to the woman's faith — a real communication of divine virtue.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ distinguishes the one believing touch ("somebody hath touched me," with design and faith, Gill) from the many accidental contacts of the crowd, and declares that healing virtue had gone out from him. Clarke draws the lesson: the divine power did not proceed from Christ automatically to all who touched him, but to this woman alone, because she came in faith — "faith alone attracts and receives the energetic influence of God." The going forth of "virtue" (Luke's medical term, Ellicott) marks a real communication of divine healing power in response to faith. The verse displays the truth that faith, not mere physical contact, draws the power of Christ: the crowd pressed and received nothing; the woman touched in faith and drew healing virtue. The same principle holds for spiritual healing: there would be more miracles of grace were there more faith (Clarke). Christ perceives and honours the believing touch; and the power that flows from him answers not the press of the multitude but the faith of the one who reaches out to him believing. Faith is the hand that draws virtue from Christ.
47:
"And when the woman saw that she was not hid, she came trembling, and falling down before him, she declared unto him before all the people for what cause she had touched him, and how she was healed immediately." The witnesses note the woman's trembling, public confession. Gill: "she hoping he would be diverted from taking any notice of her... but seeing she was not hid." Robertson: "Trembling. Vivid touch of the feeling of this sensitive woman who now had to tell everybody of her cure, in the presence of all the people. She faced the widest publicity for her secret cure."

The fathers (under v.40) note Christ's purpose in drawing out the confession. Chrysostom: "first He removes the woman's fear, lest she should suffer the pangs of conscience... Next He reproves her for thinking to lie concealed. Thirdly, He makes known her faults publicly for the sake of others." The public confession served both to confirm the miracle (the woman's own testimony) and to instruct the bystanders.

The woman's trembling confession the witnesses read as the act of obedient faith brought to light. Having sought to remain hidden, she now, finding she could not, came forward trembling and declared the whole truth before all — her disease, her touch, and her instant cure. The secret faith becomes a public testimony, glorifying Christ and edifying the crowd.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the woman, finding she could not remain hidden, came trembling and fell before Christ, declaring before all the people why she had touched him and how she was healed at once — facing the widest publicity for her secret cure (Robertson). Christ's purpose, the fathers note, was to remove her fear, reprove her concealment, and make the miracle known for others' sake (Chrysostom). The secret faith becomes a public testimony, both confirming the miracle by her own confession and instructing the bystanders. The verse displays the bringing of hidden faith to light: the woman who sought to steal a cure unseen is drawn to confess openly, glorifying Christ and edifying the crowd. Her trembling marks the sensitive humility of true faith; her public declaration, the obedience that confesses Christ before men. What was done in secret is brought to open testimony — for Christ would not leave her faith unconfessed nor her cure unknown. The trembling confession of the healed woman becomes a witness to the power and mercy of the Saviour before all the people.
48:
"And he said unto her, Daughter, be of good comfort: thy faith hath made thee whole; go in peace." The witnesses note Christ's tender, affirming word to the healed woman. Gill: "Instead of frowning upon her, and chiding her for what she had done, he addressed her in a very affable and affectionate manner." Christ, far from rebuking her secret touch, calls her "Daughter" and confirms her cure.

On "thy faith hath made thee whole," the witnesses (with the fathers under v.40) note that Christ attributes the cure to her faith. Titus Bostrensis: "He calls her daughter, as already healed because of her faith, for faith claims the grace of adoption." Origen: "He first heals her soul by faith, then truly her body." The faith that drew the healing virtue is honoured as the instrument of her cure.

On "go in peace," Ellicott refers to the like word at Luke 7:50 (the sinful woman). The dismissal in peace confirms not only the bodily healing but a deeper peace — reconciliation and rest. The woman who came trembling departs in peace, assured by Christ's own word that her faith has saved her.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse of the woman's narrative. The witnesses agree: Christ, far from rebuking the woman's secret touch, tenderly calls her "Daughter," confirms that her faith has made her whole, and dismisses her in peace. He attributes the cure to her faith (the instrument that drew the healing virtue), and by calling her "Daughter" grants the grace of adoption (Titus Bostrensis); her soul is healed by faith before her body (Origen). The "go in peace" (as to the sinful woman, Luke 7:50) confirms a peace deeper than bodily health — reconciliation and rest. The verse displays the tenderness of Christ toward trembling faith: the woman who feared rebuke receives a daughter's welcome, the assurance that her faith has saved her, and a benediction of peace. Christ honours the faith that reached out to him, names the believer his child, and sends her away whole and at peace. The trembling, secret faith of the desperate woman is met not with frowns but with the most affectionate of words — a model of how Christ receives all who, however imperfectly, reach out to him in faith. Her faith made her whole; his word made her at peace.
49:
"While he yet spake, there cometh one from the ruler of the synagogue's house, saying to him, Thy daughter is dead; trouble not the Master." The witnesses note the news of death and the timing of the delay. Chrysostom: "Our Lord conveniently waited until the death of the girl, that the miracle of her resurrection might be made public. For which reason He goes slower, and speaks longer with the woman, that the daughter may expire." The delay caused by the woman's cure was providential, allowing the greater miracle of resurrection.

A harmonization question the witnesses address. Augustine resolves the apparent conflict with Matthew (who reports Jairus saying the daughter was already dead) and Luke/Mark (who report her dying, then dead): "for the sake of brevity, Matthew chose rather to say that our Lord was asked to do what it is obvious He did, namely, to raise the dead... to those who brought that message it is not said that the father assented." The accounts agree: Christ would raise the dead.

The message "trouble not the Master" the witnesses read as the voice of unbelief. Ambrose (under the passage): "the servants of the ruler were incredulous with regard to the resurrection... as if it were impossible for Him to raise the dead." Bede reads it allegorically of those who, seeing the Synagogue dead, "do not believe it can be restored." The messengers, judging death the end, would dismiss Christ — but to him death is but sleep (v.52).

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: as Christ spoke with the woman, news came that Jairus's daughter was dead, with the counsel "trouble not the Master." The delay (caused by the woman's cure) was providential, allowing the greater miracle of resurrection to be made public (Chrysostom). Augustine harmonizes Matthew (the daughter reported already dead) with Luke/Mark (dying, then dead): the accounts agree that Christ would raise the dead. The message "trouble not the Master" is the voice of unbelief — the servants thinking resurrection impossible (Ambrose), like those who today think the dead Synagogue cannot be restored (Bede). The verse displays the testing of faith at the moment of death: the human messengers, judging death the end, would dismiss Christ; but to him death is no barrier. The interruption that seemed a tragic delay was the occasion for a greater work — not healing the sick but raising the dead, and implanting the hope of resurrection. Where human counsel says "trouble not the Master," Christ says "fear not, believe only" (v.50), for death itself is subject to him.
50:
"But when Jesus heard it, he answered him, saying, Fear not: believe only, and she shall be made whole." The witnesses note Christ's word of faith against the message of despair. The grammatical voices mark the nuance of the command. Ellicott: "There is a slight difference in the shade of meaning of the Greek tense as compared with St. Mark's report, the latter giving Believe as implying a permanent state — Be believing — St. Luke's report laying stress on the immediate act of faith." Robertson notes the added promise: "And she shall be made whole (kai sothesetai). This promise in addition to the words in Mark 5:36."

The fathers (under v.49) read Christ's strengthening of Jairus's faith. Augustine: Christ "did not blame him as distrustful, but the more strongly confirms his belief." Athanasius: "Our Lord requires faith from those who invoke Him, not because He needs the assistance of others... but not to seem to bestow His gifts according to His acceptance of persons, He shows that He favours those who believe." Bede: "if the father be willing to believe, the Synagogue also which is subject to it will be safe."

The command "fear not, believe only" the witnesses read as the answer to the message "trouble not the Master." Where the servants said death ended hope, Christ bids Jairus believe and promises wholeness. The verse calls for faith precisely at the point where human hope fails — the moment of death — and promises that faith will see the dead raised.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: against the message of despair ("thy daughter is dead"), Christ bids Jairus "fear not, believe only," and promises she shall be made whole — Luke stressing the immediate act of faith (Ellicott) and adding the promise of wholeness (Robertson). Christ does not blame Jairus's wavering but strengthens his faith (Augustine), requiring faith not from need but that his gifts may be received and held (Athanasius). The command answers the servants' "trouble not the Master": where they said death ended hope, Christ bids the father believe and promises resurrection. The verse displays Christ's call to faith precisely where human hope fails — at the moment of death itself. To the grieving father, Christ speaks the word that sustains all believers in the face of death: fear not, believe only. Faith is summoned not when the case is easy but when it is humanly hopeless; and Christ, who is the resurrection and the life, promises that such faith will see the dead made whole. Where death has spoken its word of despair, Christ speaks his word of faith and life.
51:
"And when he came into the house, he suffered no man to go in, save Peter, and James, and John, and the father and the mother of the maiden." The witnesses note the restricted company at the raising. Ellicott: "St. Luke agrees with St. Mark in giving the names; St. Matthew omits them. St. Mark states more definitely that none others were allowed even to go with Him." Christ admitted only the three chief disciples and the parents.

The fathers (under v.49) read the restriction as a lesson in humility. Theophylact: "When He was about to raise the dead He put all out, as teaching us to be free from vain-glory, and to do nothing for show... he must not be in the midst of a great many, but alone." Chrysostom: he took Peter, James, and John "as the Heads of His disciples, and able to conceal the miracle. For He did not wish to be revealed to many before His time, perhaps on account of the envy of the Jews."

The chosen witnesses the witnesses note as the same three admitted to the Transfiguration and Gethsemane — the inner circle, chosen to be credible witnesses to the resurrection. Chrysostom: "He took also the parents as witnesses, lest any should say the evidence of the resurrection was false." The restriction both teaches humility (avoiding ostentation) and secures reliable testimony.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ admitted only Peter, James, and John (the inner circle, named by Luke and Mark, Ellicott) and the parents to the raising. The fathers read the restriction as a lesson in humility — putting all out, doing nothing for show or vain-glory (Theophylact) — and as securing credible witnesses, the chief disciples able to conceal the miracle before his time, and the parents lest the resurrection be thought false (Chrysostom). The same three were admitted to the Transfiguration and Gethsemane. The verse displays Christ's avoidance of ostentation and his care for reliable testimony: the greatest miracle is wrought not before a crowd but before a few chosen witnesses. Christ would not make a spectacle of raising the dead, nor reveal himself prematurely to the envious; yet he ensured that trustworthy witnesses — the inner three and the parents — should attest the resurrection. The verse teaches both the humility that shuns display and the wisdom that secures sufficient testimony to a work of divine power.
52:
"And all wept, and bewailed her: but he said, Weep not; she is not dead, but sleepeth." The witnesses note the mourning and Christ's word that death is but sleep. The Reformed Calvin reads the lamentation as confirming the death and exposing Judean corruptions: "The Evangelists mention the lamentation, that the resurrection may be more fully believed... the flute was heard in plaintive airs... we see how strongly inclined the world is not only to indulge but to promote its faults... whence we may infer how much the country of Judea was then filled with many corruptions." The hired mourners and flutes both attest the real death and display the disordered, even pagan, customs of grief.

On "she is not dead, but sleepeth," the witnesses note Christ's transformation of death into sleep. Wesley: "Her soul is not separated finally from the body; and this short separation is rather to be called sleep than death." Chrysostom (under v.49): Christ spoke thus "showing that all things were at His command, and that He would bring her to life as if He were awakening her from sleep." To Christ, who would raise her, death is no more than sleep.

The mourning the witnesses read as both genuine and, in its hired excess, a mark of unbelief. The mourners wept as for one finally lost; Christ, knowing he would raise her, called it sleep. The contrast between the wailing crowd and Christ's calm word displays the difference between the world's despair before death and the faith that sees death, for the believer, as sleep.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the mourners (relatives and hired wailers with flutes) wept and bewailed the girl, but Christ said "she is not dead, but sleepeth." Calvin reads the lamentation as both confirming the real death (so the resurrection be more believed) and exposing the disordered, near-pagan customs of grief in corrupt Judea. Christ's word transforms death into sleep: to him who would raise her, the separation of soul and body is but a short sleep (Wesley), and he would awaken her as from slumber (Chrysostom). The verse displays the difference between the world's despair and Christ's calm mastery over death: the crowd wails as for one finally lost, but Christ, the Lord of life, calls death sleep. For the believer, death is no longer the end but a sleep from which Christ will wake him. The mourning that attests the real death only magnifies the coming miracle; and Christ's serene "she sleepeth" proclaims his authority over death itself, which to him is but a slumber he can end with a word.
53:
"And they laughed him to scorn, knowing that she was dead." The witnesses note the mockery of the mourners and its evidential value. Gill: "The servants, neighbours, and relations, the pipers, and mourning women: these, from weeping for the dead, fell to laughing at Christ, having him and his words in the utmost derision." Robertson: "Knowing that she was dead (eidotes hoti apethanen). That she died, second aorist active indicative."

The mockery the witnesses read as ironic confirmation of the real death. The mourners, certain the girl was truly dead, scorned Christ's word that she slept. Chrysostom (under v.49): "He did not reprove them nor put an end to their laughter, that laughter also might be a sign of death. For since generally, after a miracle has been performed men continue unfaithful, He takes them by their own words." Their scornful certainty that she was dead becomes the proof that the coming raising is a true resurrection, not the rousing of one merely faint.

The transition from weeping to laughing the witnesses note as exposing the shallowness of the hired grief and the unbelief of the crowd. Ambrose (under v.49): "whoever believes not, laughs." The same people who wailed now mock — their grief and their scorn alike marks of a faithlessness that cannot conceive of resurrection.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the mourners, knowing the girl was truly dead, laughed Christ to scorn for saying she slept. Their derision is the ironic confirmation of the real death: their scornful certainty that she was dead (Robertson) becomes the proof that the coming raising is a true resurrection (Chrysostom). Christ did not silence their laughter, that it might stand as witness to her death. The swift turn from weeping to mocking exposes the shallowness of the hired grief and the unbelief of the crowd — for whoever believes not, laughs (Ambrose). The verse displays the scorn of unbelief in the face of Christ's word: the crowd, sure of death, ridicules the Lord of life. Yet their mockery serves the truth, certifying that she was indeed dead and that her raising would be no mere recovery but a resurrection. The laughter of unbelief becomes, against its will, a testimony to the greatness of the miracle Christ was about to perform. Those who scorned his word would soon see it vindicated.
54:
"And he put them all out, and took her by the hand, and called, saying, Maid, arise." The witnesses note Christ's clearing of the room, his touch, and his word of power. Clarke: "He put them all out — that is, the pipers and those who made a noise, weeping and lamenting... Pompous funeral ceremonies are ridiculous in themselves, and entirely opposed to the spirit and simplicity of the religion of Christ." The mocking, faithless crowd is excluded from the miracle.

The grammatical voices note the touch and the call. Robertson: "Called (ephonesen). Certainly not to wake up the dead, but to make it plain to all that she rose in response to his elevated tone of voice. The touch of Christ's hand and the power of his voice restored her to life." On whether she was really dead or in a trance, Robertson: "It matters little. The touch of Christ's hand and the power of his voice restored her to life."

The raising the witnesses read as the act of divine power. Christ took her by the hand and called "Maid, arise" — the touch and the word together restoring life. Chrysostom (under v.49): "He poured not into her another soul, but restored the same which she had breathed forth." The simple word and gentle touch of Christ, addressed to the dead as to the sleeping, raised her at once.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ put out the mocking crowd, took the dead girl by the hand, and called "Maid, arise" — the touch of his hand and the power of his voice restoring her to life (Robertson). The faithless mourners are excluded; the pompous, near-pagan funeral customs have no place in the presence of the Lord of life (Clarke). The call was made not to wake the dead (who cannot hear) but that all might see she rose in response to his word; and Christ restored the very soul she had breathed forth, not another (Chrysostom). Whether she was truly dead or (as some suppose) in a trance matters little — the touch and word of Christ restored her (Robertson), though the mourners' certainty of death (v.53) and Christ's own command confirm a true resurrection. The verse displays the divine power of Christ over death: with a gentle touch and a simple word, addressed to the dead as to one asleep, he raises the girl to life. The Lord who calls the dead to arise is the resurrection and the life; and his word, spoken to a corpse, is answered by the return of life.
55:
"And her spirit came again, and she arose straightway: and he commanded to give her meat." The witnesses note the return of the girl's spirit and Christ's command to feed her. Gill draws the doctrine from "her spirit came again": "Her soul, which was departed from her... returned to her body, and re-entered: this shows that the soul is immortal, and dies not with the body; that it exists in a separate state from it after death, and will hereafter re-enter the body... where her spirit was during this time of separation, is needless, and would be curious and rash to inquire." Ellicott notes the precise phrase "is peculiar to St. Luke, and is characteristic of the more accurate phraseology that belonged to him as a physician."

On "commanded to give her meat," Clarke draws a rich lesson: "Though she was raised to life by a miracle, she was not to be preserved by a miracle. Nature is God's great instrument, and he delights to work by it; nor will he do anything by his sovereign power, in the way of miracle, that can be effected by his ordinary providence. Again, God will have us be workers together with him: he provides food for us, but he does not eat for us." He applies it to salvation: "God provides salvation for a lost world, and bestows it on every penitent believing soul; but he neither repents nor believes for any man."

The return of the spirit and the command to feed the witnesses read as confirming the reality of the resurrection. Chrysostom (under v.49): "he commanded to give her meat, that it might not seem like a vision what was done." The eating proved she was truly alive, restored to ordinary human life, not a phantom.

The witnesses agree: at Christ's word the girl's spirit returned and she rose at once, and Christ commanded that she be given food. The return of the spirit, Luke's precise physician's phrase (Ellicott), attests the immortality of the soul, its separate existence after death, and its reunion with the body (Gill). The command to feed her both confirms the reality of the resurrection (she truly lives, not a phantom, Chrysostom) and teaches that God works by ordinary means where miracle is not needed — nature is his instrument, and he makes us workers together with him (Clarke). The verse displays the completeness and reality of the raising: the soul restored, the body risen, the girl returned to ordinary life and nourished by ordinary food. Christ raises by miracle but sustains by providence; and the same God who provides salvation will have us receive and use it, even as the raised girl must eat the food provided. The resurrection is real, the life restored is true human life, and the Lord who gives life also appoints the ordinary means by which it is sustained.
56:
"And her parents were astonished: but he charged them that they should tell no man what was done." The witnesses note the parents' amazement and Christ's charge to silence. Gill notes their astonishment at the miracle; Barnes refers to the parallel accounts. The parents, seeing their dead daughter restored, were overcome with wonder.

The charge to silence the witnesses (with the Greek Exposition, under v.49) read as Christ's avoidance of vain-glory. The Greek Exposition: "showing that He is the Giver of good things, but not covetous of glory, and that He gives the whole, receiving nothing. But he who seeks after the glory of his works has indeed shown forth something, but receives something." Christ, who gives freely and seeks no glory for himself, charges the parents not to publish the miracle.

The charge also the witnesses note as prudence regarding the time and the envy of the Jews. Chrysostom (under v.49): "He did not wish to be revealed to many before His time, perhaps on account of the envy of the Jews." The premature spreading of such a miracle would inflame opposition and provoke a popular movement before the appointed time. Christ's charge restrains the publicity that would hinder his purpose.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse, which closes the chapter. The witnesses agree: the parents, astonished at their daughter's resurrection, were charged by Christ to tell no man what was done. The charge reflects Christ's avoidance of vain-glory — he gives freely and seeks no glory for himself (Greek Exposition) — and his prudence regarding the time and the envy of the Jews, not wishing to be revealed to many before his hour (Chrysostom). The verse displays the humility and wisdom of Christ in his miracles: having raised the dead, he charges silence, refusing to court the fame that such a wonder would bring, and restraining the publicity that would prematurely inflame opposition or provoke a popular movement. The Lord who raises the dead seeks not his own glory but the Father's purpose, and works in a manner that, even in its greatest displays of power, shuns ostentation. The raising of Jairus's daughter — a pledge of the resurrection and a proof of Christ's authority over death — closes not with a public proclamation but with a charge to silence, that the work might speak for itself and the glory be God's alone. The Giver of life receives nothing for himself but gives the whole.
